title: HP and Intel's Cautionary Arc: What Happens After the Founders Walk Away

source.original: David Senra's Conversation with Marc Andreessen (@pmarca) – Founders, Tech as Progress, and the Zero Introspection Mindset

body_html:
When Andreessen reaches for historical examples of the founder-to-manager transition, Hewlett-Packard and Intel aren't arbitrary picks. Both companies were once synonymous with relentless innovation—HP essentially defined Silicon Valley's culture, and Intel's founding team created the microprocessor revolution. Both eventually became case studies in institutional entropy.

HP's trajectory is particularly instructive. Under Bill Hewlett and Dave Packard, the company operated with a distinctive ethos often called "The HP Way"—an engineering-first culture that prized technical excellence and employee autonomy. As successive waves of professional management took over, that culture was systematically diluted. By the time the company was cycling through CEOs in the 2000s, the HP Way was little more than a marketing artifact.

Intel followed a related but distinct path. Under Andy Grove, the company made one of the most famous strategic pivots in business history, abandoning memory chips for microprocessors. That decision required founder-level conviction—the willingness to bet the entire company on a thesis that most of the organization resisted. It's nearly impossible to imagine a career manager making that call.

The lesson Andreessen draws from both examples isn't that managers are villains. It's that certain kinds of decisions can only be made by people with founder-level conviction and risk tolerance. When those people leave and are replaced by stewards, the company's strategic ceiling drops permanently, even if short-term performance improves.

These aren't just business school anecdotes—they're warnings. Every company that outlives its founders faces this inflection point, and very few navigate it without losing the essence of what made them exceptional.
